clue: On a bare limestone ridge crowning a rain-watered plateau at the headwaters of a great river system, late stone-age hunter-gatherers quarried and erected colossal T-shaped pillars carved with reliefs of animals. The site demonstrates that organised ritual and monumental construction preceded the invention of agriculture by millennia, overturning long-held assumptions about the preconditions for complex society.

On a low twin mound rising from a broad interior plain drained by a sluggish river, one of the ancient world's earliest large settlements flourished for over a thousand years. Its inhabitants entered their homes through holes in the roof rather than street-level doors, buried their dead beneath the earthen floors, and covered their walls in vivid painted scenes of hunts and herds. The densely packed mud-brick dwellings shared walls on all sides, leaving no streets between them.

Where a long, narrow river valley gives way to a broad marshy delta, a ruler unified two ancient crowns for the first time — a red crown of the lowland wetlands and a white crown of the upstream desert corridor — and founded a capital city at the boundary between them. The act inaugurated one of the ancient world's longest-lasting and most prolific civilisations, whose monumental stone works still dominate the surrounding desert.

locationName: Ziggurat of Ur, Dhi Qar Province, Iraq
clue: On the flat alluvial plain between the two great rivers of the ancient world, the ruler of a powerful city-state directed the construction of a massive three-tiered mudbrick platform. Broad ceremonial staircases rose to a temple at its summit, dominating a city whose royal cemetery — excavated in the modern era — yielded golden headdresses, lyres, and evidence of elaborate retainer burial rites that accompanied the deaths of its monarchs.

In the broad flood plain of a great northwestern river system, Bronze Age city planners laid out streets on a strict cardinal grid of fired brick, equipping them with a covered underground drainage network more sophisticated than anything in the contemporary world. At the heart of the upper citadel stood a large communal bathing tank fed by wells and lined with bitumen-sealed brickwork. The civilisation's undeciphered script has resisted every attempt at translation.
